quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conservation of momentum"?

options.text: E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit
A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy
x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles
p_total,initial = p_total,final; total momentum of an isolated system remains constant

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conservation of momentum" is defined as: p_total,initial = p_total,final; total momentum of an isolated system remains constant. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Isolated system"?

options.text: A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs

quiz_questions.explanation: "Isolated system" is defined as: A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Internal vs. external forces"?

options.text: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
Internal forces (between system objects) don't change total momentum; external forces (from outside the system) do
x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles

quiz_questions.explanation: "Internal vs. external forces" is defined as: Internal forces (between system objects) don't change total momentum; external forces (from outside the system) do. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Two-dimensional momentum conservation"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
Momentum is conserved independently in the x and y directions: Σp_x,i = Σp_x,f and Σp_y,i = Σp_y,f

quiz_questions.explanation: "Two-dimensional momentum conservation" is defined as: Momentum is conserved independently in the x and y directions: Σp_x,i = Σp_x,f and Σp_y,i = Σp_y,f. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Recoil"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
When an object ejects mass (gun firing, rocket exhaust), the remaining object gains momentum in the opposite direction
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Recoil" is defined as: When an object ejects mass (gun firing, rocket exhaust), the remaining object gains momentum in the opposite direction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "p_total,initial = p_total,final; total momentum of an isolated system remains constant"?

options.text: Hooke's Law
Angular position (θ)
Conservation of momentum
Dot product

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conservation of momentum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law"?

options.text: Gravitational field
System of equations
Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Isolated system

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Isolated system".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Internal forces (between system objects) don't change total momentum; external forces (from outside the system) do"?

options.text: Newton's First Law
Work by a spring
Internal vs. external forces
Hooke's Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Internal vs. external forces".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Momentum is conserved independently in the x and y directions: Σp_x,i = Σp_x,f and Σp_y,i = Σp_y,f"?

options.text: Net force
Two-dimensional momentum conservation
Efficiency
Potential energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Two-dimensional momentum conservation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When an object ejects mass (gun firing, rocket exhaust), the remaining object gains momentum in the opposite direction"?

options.text: Potential energy
Recoil
Simple pendulum period
Vertical circle dynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Recoil".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Conservation of Momentum is accurate?

options.text: Real collisions are partially inelastic.
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.
For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².
Conservation of Momentum states that when no external forces act on a system, the total momentum is constant: Σp_initial = Σp_final.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.
This principle is derived directly from Newton's Third Law—internal forces between objects cancel in pairs.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: Real collisions are partially inelastic.

Conservation of momentum applies to collisions, explosions, and recoil in one and two dimensions, and is particularly powerful when forces are impulsive (large and brief) or when the system is isolated.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.
Conservation of Momentum states that when no external forces act on a system, the total momentum is constant: Σp_initial = Σp_final.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: This principle is derived directly from Newton's Third Law—internal forces between objects cancel in pairs.

The centripetal force is not a new type of force—it is the net radial component of real forces (tension, gravity, normal force, friction) that provides the inward acceleration needed to maintain circular motion.
In perfectly inelastic collisions, the objects stick together and maximum KE is lost (converted to thermal/sound energy).
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservation of Momentum as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Firearms forensics using conservation of momentum to calculate bullet speed from the recoil velocity of a ballistic pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Conservation of Momentum applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments
Space debris collision modeling using momentum conservation to predict trajectories of fragments for satellite safety planning
Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides
Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Conservation of Momentum?

options.text: Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Sports equipment design (golf clubs, tennis rackets) optimizing mass ratios and coefficients of restitution for maximum energy transfer to the ball
Firearms forensics using conservation of momentum to calculate bullet speed from the recoil velocity of a ballistic pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Conservation of Momentum in practice?

options.text: Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Structural engineering resolving forces on bridge cables into horizontal and vertical components to analyze tension and compression in each member
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles
Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservation of Momentum.
